era_context: The Akkadian Empire is in its final years or has just collapsed (dating is uncertain). Egypt's Sixth Dynasty has ended, and the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE) is beginning. The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its mature peak. This is a year of major political transition in Mesopotamia and Egypt.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: collapse or final fragmentation; transition to city-state period
events.description: The Akkadian Empire either collapses or is in its final stages of fragmentation around this time (dating is uncertain; some scholars place the collapse c. 2218 BCE, others c. 2200 BCE or later). Naram-Sin's successor, Shar-kali-sharri (r. c. 2217–2193 BCE or later), rules over a rapidly fragmenting empire. Within a few years, Akkadian central authority will cease to exist, and Mesopotamia will return to a period of independent city-states. The Guti, a highland people, may have played a role in the empire's collapse, though their exact role is debated.
key_figures: Guti (highland people, possibly involved in Akkad's collapse)

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts
The Curse of Akkad (later Sumerian composition)
Historiographic consensus on Akkad's collapse (Liverani, Postgate)

events.certainty_note: The Akkadian collapse is dated c. 2218–2200 BCE by various scholars, with uncertainty of ±10–20 years. Shar-kali-sharri's reign is attested in inscriptions, but his exact regnal dates are debated. The role of the Guti in the collapse is disputed.
cross_references: Leads to -2100-001 (Guti period and city-state fragmentation)

events.title: Egypt: First Intermediate Period begins; political fragmentation and regional autonomy
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty has ended, and the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE) has begun. Egypt is now fragmented into competing regional powers. Nomarchs in Upper Egypt and the Delta rule as independent lords. The concept of a unified Egyptian state has temporarily ceased to exist. This period will last approximately 125 years until the Middle Kingdom's reunification under the Eleventh Dynasty.
key_figures: Various nomarchs and regional rulers (names and titles vary by region)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of regional autonomy and reduced state construction
Abydos King List and Turin Papyrus (later compilations)

events.certainty_note: The First Intermediate Period is archaeologically well-attested. The conventional dating of its beginning is c. 2181 BCE, though this is a scholarly convention rather than a precisely dated event.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: mature phase continues; no evidence of disruption
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains stable and prosperous. Urban centres continue to function, trade networks remain active, and there is no evidence of disruption or decline. The civilization will continue in this state for another 200+ years.

sources.name: Archaeological layers and artifacts from Indus sites

events.certainty_note: The mature Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested.
cross_references: Contrasts with contemporary collapse in Mesopotamia and Egypt

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the Akkadian collapse is highly debated. Some scholars place it c. 2218 BCE (end of Naram-Sin's reign); others place it c. 2200 BCE or even later. The role of the Guti in the collapse is disputed; some scholars argue they were a consequence rather than a cause of the empire's fragmentation. The Curse of Akkad is a later literary composition and should not be treated as a contemporary historical account. The conventional dating of the First Intermediate Period's beginning (c. 2181 BCE) is a scholarly convention, not a precisely dated event.
historiographic_note: This year marks a major transition in both Mesopotamia and Egypt, but the precise dating of these transitions is uncertain (±10–20 years). The Akkadian collapse and the First Intermediate Period are documented through inscriptions, administrative texts, and archaeology, but absolute chronology depends on synchronisms and conventional boundaries. Western scholarship has historically emphasized the Akkadian collapse as a major civilizational event; the First Intermediate Period was long viewed as a period of 'chaos' and decline, though modern scholarship recognizes it as a period of regional autonomy and cultural continuity. The Indus Valley Civilization's apparent resilience to the climate stress and political upheaval affecting Mesopotamia and Egypt remains poorly understood.

graph_edges.note: Akkad's collapse led to the Guti period and the fragmentation of Mesopotamia into independent city-states.
The First Intermediate Period lasted approximately 125 years before the Middle Kingdom's reunification.
Both Mesopotamia and Egypt experienced major political collapse and fragmentation around 2200–2125 BCE, suggesting possible regional or global factors.
